A Giffen good is strongly inferior, so the fall in real income raises demand enough that the positive income effect dominates. A has the substitution sign wrong. C describes a normal good. D has both signs wrong.

questions.question: Suppose a staple food is inferior. When its price falls, which condition would produce Giffen behavior?

choices.A: Demand rises because both substitution and income effects are positive.
choices.B: Demand falls because the negative income effect from higher real purchasing power is larger than the positive substitution effect.
choices.C: Demand stays constant because inferior goods always have offsetting effects.
choices.D: Demand rises because the income effect is zero.

questions.explanation: For a price fall, substitution increases demand. For an inferior good, the real-income increase reduces demand. Giffen behavior means demand moves in the same direction as price, so demand falls when price falls; the negative income effect must dominate. A describes a normal good. C assumes exact cancellation. D ignores the defining income effect.

questions.question: In a demand graph with p_x vertical and x horizontal, the demand curve for low-grade rice slopes upward over a range from p=2 to p=3. What does this slope mean in terms of substitution and income effects?

choices.A: The positive income effect of the price increase must exceed the negative substitution effect.
choices.B: The substitution effect must be positive because the curve slopes upward.
choices.C: The good must be normal because consumers spend more on it.
choices.D: The budget line must shift outward when price rises.

questions.explanation: An upward-sloping ordinary demand curve means quantity rises with price. Since own-price substitution is negative for a price rise, a larger positive income effect is required. B misstates substitution. C confuses expenditure with normality. D reverses the real-income effect.

questions.question: A Slutsky diagram for a price increase of x shows A to B moving left by 3 units due to substitution and B to C moving right by 5 units due to the income effect. Where is final point C relative to A?

choices.A: 2 units to the right of A, showing Giffen behavior.
choices.B: 8 units to the left of A, showing ordinary demand.
choices.C: 3 units to the left of A, showing no income effect.
choices.D: 5 units to the right of A, showing only income effect.

questions.explanation: The total movement is -3+5=+2, so final demand is 2 units higher after a price increase. That is Giffen behavior. B adds absolute values incorrectly. C ignores the income effect. D ignores substitution.

questions.question: Why must a Giffen good be inferior but not every inferior good is Giffen?

choices.A: Giffen behavior requires a positive income effect from inferiority large enough to outweigh the negative substitution effect; many inferior goods have income effects too small to dominate.
choices.B: Giffen behavior requires the substitution effect to be positive; inferior goods have negative substitution effects.
choices.C: Giffen goods are inferior only when prices fall, but ordinary inferior goods are inferior only when income rises.
choices.D: All inferior goods are Giffen by definition.

questions.explanation: Inferiority gives the income effect the direction needed for Giffen behavior, but it must be large enough to outweigh substitution. B gets substitution wrong. C invents separate definitions. D states the common error.

questions.question: A poor household spends most of its food budget on a cheap staple. When the staple's price rises, the household can no longer afford meat and buys even more of the staple despite its higher price. Which model best explains this?

choices.A: Giffen-good demand, where a large positive income effect dominates substitution.
choices.B: Normal-good demand, where higher prices increase willingness to pay.
choices.C: Perfect complements, where the household must buy meat and staple in fixed proportions.
choices.D: WARP violation, because the household changed its bundle.

questions.explanation: The staple's price increase lowers real purchasing power so strongly that the household substitutes away from higher-quality foods toward more staple. That is Giffen behavior. B contradicts normal demand. C is inconsistent with abandoning meat. D mistakes changed choices for inconsistency.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes an inferior good from a Giffen good?

choices.A: An inferior good has demand that falls when income rises; a Giffen good has demand that rises when its own price rises because the income effect dominates substitution.
choices.B: An inferior good has upward-sloping demand; a Giffen good has downward-sloping demand.
choices.C: An inferior good violates utility maximization; a Giffen good satisfies it.
choices.D: An inferior good has no substitution effect; a Giffen good has no income effect.

questions.explanation: Inferiority concerns income response; Giffen behavior concerns own-price response. B reverses the usual distinction. C falsely says inferior goods violate optimization. D falsely removes the two effects.

questions.question: A Giffen good has ordinary demand x=10+2p over prices 2 to 4. If price rises from 2 to 4, quantity rises from 14 to 18. Why is calculating ordinary consumer-surplus loss from this upward-sloping demand problematic?

choices.A: Because ordinary demand includes income effects; for welfare from a price change, compensated demand is the appropriate exact object.
choices.B: Because consumer surplus is always negative for Giffen goods.
choices.C: Because the quantity increase means consumers must be better off after the price rise.
choices.D: Because Giffen goods do not have utility functions.

questions.explanation: Ordinary demand for a Giffen good slopes upward because income effects dominate, so the area under Marshallian demand is not an exact welfare measure. Compensated demand is needed for exact welfare measures such as compensating variation. B, C, and D are false.

questions.question: Riley is endowed with 10 apples and 5 bananas. Prices are p_a=2 and p_b=4. Utility is U(a,b)=a^{1/2}b^{1/2}. What is Riley's gross demand bundle?

choices.A: a=10, b=5 because the consumer keeps the endowment.
choices.B: a=10, b=5 because equal shares reproduce the endowment.
choices.C: a=10, b=5 because the endowment value is 40 and demands are a=20/2=10, b=20/4=5.
choices.D: a=20, b=10 because endowment quantities double through trade.

questions.explanation: The endowment value is 2(10)+4(5)=40. Equal-share Cobb-Douglas demands are a=20/2=10 and b=20/4=5. Here the optimum equals the endowment, but for the reason in C. A assumes no trade always. B does not show the budget-value logic. D violates the budget.

questions.question: Sam has endowment omega=(12 units of x, 0 units of y), prices p_x=5 and p_y=10, and utility U=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}. What is Sam's optimal gross demand and net trade?

choices.A: Gross demand x=6, y=3; net trade is sell 6 units of x and buy 3 units of y.
choices.B: Gross demand x=12, y=0; net trade is zero because income is not given.
choices.C: Gross demand x=3, y=6; net trade is sell 9 units of x and buy 6 units of y.
choices.D: Gross demand x=0, y=6; net trade is sell all x and buy 6 units of y.

questions.explanation: Endowment value is 5(12)=60. Equal-share demands are x=30/5=6 and y=30/10=3. Relative to omega=(12,0), Sam sells 6 units of x and buys 3 y. B ignores the value of the endowment. C swaps price denominators. D is a corner not optimal for Cobb-Douglas.

questions.question: Taylor owns an endowment of omega=(10 units of x, 10 units of y). Initially p_x=2 and p_y=2. Then p_x rises to 4 while p_y stays 2. What happens to the value of Taylor's endowment, and what mechanism is unique to the endowment model?

choices.A: Endowment value rises from 40 to 60; because Taylor owns x, the price increase raises wealth as well as changing relative prices.
choices.B: Endowment value falls from 40 to 30; higher prices reduce purchasing power for everyone.
choices.C: Endowment value stays 40; income is fixed in consumer theory.
choices.D: Endowment value rises only if Taylor buys x, not if Taylor owns it.

questions.explanation: Initial value is 2(10)+2(10)=40. New value is 4(10)+2(10)=60. In an endowment model, a price change affects both relative prices and the value of owned resources. B ignores ownership. C applies fixed-income logic. D reverses the source of wealth change.

questions.question: Jordan is a net seller of x. If p_x rises while p_y is fixed, what wealth effect does Jordan experience, and why?

choices.A: A positive wealth effect, because the value of Jordan's endowment of x rises more than the cost of the x Jordan consumes.
choices.B: A negative wealth effect, because higher p_x always hurts consumers.
choices.C: No wealth effect, because price changes only rotate budget lines.
choices.D: A positive substitution effect, because selling x is the same as consuming x.

questions.explanation: A net seller owns more x than they consume. A higher price of x raises the value of what they sell, creating a positive wealth effect. B is fixed-income reasoning. C ignores endowment-value changes. D confuses selling with consuming and mislabels the effect.

questions.question: In an endowment diagram with x horizontal and y vertical, the budget line always passes through the endowment point omega. If p_x rises holding p_y fixed, how does the budget line change?

choices.A: It becomes steeper and rotates through the endowment point.
choices.B: It shifts inward in parallel because real income falls.
choices.C: It shifts outward in parallel because wealth rises.
choices.D: It becomes flatter and rotates through the y-intercept.

questions.explanation: The endowment budget line is p_x x+p_y y=p_x omega_x+p_y omega_y and passes through omega. A rise in p_x makes slope -p_x/p_y steeper and rotates the line through omega. B and C are fixed-income parallel-shift reasoning. D gives the wrong slope change and pivot.

questions.question: A consumer's endowment point lies to the right of her chosen bundle in an x-y diagram. What does the horizontal distance between the endowment and chosen bundle represent?

choices.A: Net supply of x, because she consumes less x than she owns.
choices.B: Gross demand for x, because it measures total x consumed.
choices.C: Income effect, because the budget line shifted.
choices.D: Consumer surplus, because she sells x.

questions.explanation: If the endowment has more x than the chosen bundle, the consumer sells the difference. That horizontal distance is net supply of x. B would be the chosen x-coordinate itself. C is not a distance between endowment and choice. D confuses net trade with welfare.

questions.question: Why does a price increase of a good have a different effect on a consumer who is endowed with that good than on a consumer with fixed money income?

choices.A: Because the price increase changes both the opportunity cost of consuming the good and the market value of the consumer's endowment.
choices.B: Because endowed consumers do not face budget constraints.
choices.C: Because fixed-income consumers cannot substitute between goods.
choices.D: Because endowed consumers always gain from price increases.

questions.explanation: In endowment problems, prices determine the value of the endowment, so price changes affect wealth as well as relative prices. B is false. C is false. D is too strong because net buyers can be hurt.

questions.question: A farmer grows 100 bushels of corn and consumes some corn and other goods. When the corn price changes, her purchasing power changes because she can sell corn. Which framework should model her choice?

choices.A: A fixed-income consumer problem, because prices are given.
choices.B: An endowment problem, because her budget comes from the market value of what she owns.
choices.C: A monopoly problem, because she sells corn.
choices.D: A public-good problem, because corn is food.

questions.explanation: The farmer's budget is generated by selling an initial endowment of corn, so the endowment framework is appropriate. A misses the wealth effect of price changes. C requires market power, not merely selling. D is irrelevant.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes fixed-income consumer choice from endowment consumer choice?

choices.A: In fixed-income choice, money income is exogenous; in endowment choice, income equals the market value of the initial bundle and changes with prices.
choices.B: In fixed-income choice, consumers optimize; in endowment choice, they keep their initial bundle.
choices.C: In fixed-income choice, prices matter; in endowment choice, prices do not matter.
choices.D: In fixed-income choice, demand is gross; in endowment choice, demand is always zero.

questions.explanation: Endowment income is p dot omega, which changes when prices change. Fixed money income is exogenous. B falsely says endowed consumers never trade. C is false. D confuses gross and net demand.

questions.question: Kai has endowment omega=(10,0), prices initially p_x=2 and p_y=1, and utility U=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}. If p_x rises to 4, what happens to endowment wealth and why is this not by itself consumer surplus?

choices.A: Wealth rises from 20 to 40; this is a budget-value change, not surplus area under a demand curve.
choices.B: Wealth falls from 20 to 10; this is deadweight loss.
choices.C: Wealth stays 20; consumer surplus rises by 20.
choices.D: Wealth rises from 10 to 40; the gain equals producer surplus.

questions.explanation: The endowment value rises from 2(10)=20 to 4(10)=40. This topic does not directly require welfare analysis; the Type 7 slot is used to calculate endowment wealth and distinguish it from surplus. B has the wrong direction. C ignores price-value changes. D miscalculates initial wealth and mislabels the concept.

questions.question: A consumer has endowment omega=(8 units of x, 4 units of y), prices p_x=3 and p_y=6, and utility U=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}. What are gross demand and net demand?

choices.A: Gross demand is (8,4); net demand is (0,0).
choices.B: Gross demand is (8,4); net demand is (8,4).
choices.C: Gross demand is (4,8); net demand is (-4,4).
choices.D: Gross demand is (12,6); net demand is (4,2).

questions.explanation: Endowment value is 3(8)+6(4)=48. Equal-share demand gives x=24/3=8 and y=24/6=4. Gross demand is the chosen bundle (8,4), and net demand is gross minus endowment, (0,0). B confuses gross with net. C swaps demands. D violates the budget.

questions.question: Bianca has endowment omega=(20,0), prices p_x=2, p_y=5, and utility U=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}. What is her gross demand and net demand?

choices.A: Gross demand (20,0); net demand (0,0).
choices.B: Gross demand (10,4); net demand (-10,4).
choices.C: Gross demand (4,10); net demand (-16,10).
choices.D: Gross demand (0,8); net demand (-20,8).

questions.explanation: Endowment value is 2(20)=40. Equal shares imply x=20/2=10 and y=20/5=4. Net demand is (10-20,4-0)=(-10,4), meaning she sells 10 units of x and buys 4 units of y. A assumes no trade. C swaps price denominators. D is a corner.

questions.question: At current prices, Carlos's gross demand for x is 6 and his endowment of x is 10. If p_x rises and his gross demand becomes 7 while his endowment is unchanged, what happens to his net demand for x and what mechanism may explain it?

choices.A: Net demand rises from -4 to -3; as a net seller, the higher price can increase wealth enough to raise gross consumption of x.
choices.B: Net demand falls from 4 to 3; he becomes a net buyer.
choices.C: Net demand stays zero because endowment is unchanged.
choices.D: Net demand rises from 6 to 7 because net demand equals gross demand.

questions.explanation: Net demand is gross demand minus endowment. Initially 6-10=-4; after the price change it is 7-10=-3. He remains a net seller but sells less. The wealth effect from owning x may raise gross consumption. B has the sign wrong. C ignores gross demand changes. D confuses gross and net.

questions.question: Dana's endowment of concert tickets x is 2. At p_x=20 her gross demand is 5. At p_x=40 her gross demand is 3. What happens to her net position?

choices.A: She remains a net buyer, but net demand falls from 3 to 1.
choices.B: She becomes a net seller because price rose.
choices.C: Her net demand rises from 2 to 3.
choices.D: Her gross demand is unchanged because endowment is fixed.

questions.explanation: Net demand is x^d-omega_x. Initially 5-2=3; finally 3-2=1. She still buys tickets but fewer. B assumes any price rise makes owners sellers. C confuses gross demand with endowment. D ignores demand response.

questions.question: On an endowment diagram, the chosen bundle lies to the left of the endowment point but above it. What does this imply about net demands?

choices.A: Net demand for x is negative and net demand for y is positive.
choices.B: Gross demand for both goods is negative.
choices.C: Net demand for both goods is positive.
choices.D: The consumer is not trading because the chosen bundle is near the endowment.

questions.explanation: Left of the endowment means chosen x is less than endowed x, so net demand for x is negative. Above the endowment means chosen y is greater than endowed y, so net demand for y is positive. B is impossible for ordinary goods. C gets x wrong. D ignores the displacement from the endowment.

questions.question: In a diagram, the horizontal coordinate of the chosen bundle is 14 and the horizontal coordinate of the endowment is 9. What does the horizontal gap represent?

choices.A: Gross demand of 5 units of x.
choices.B: Net demand of +5 units of x, meaning the consumer buys 5 units of x.
choices.C: Net supply of 14 units of x.
choices.D: Income effect of 9 units of x.

questions.explanation: Net demand is chosen quantity minus endowment quantity: 14-9=+5. A confuses the gap with gross demand; gross demand is 14. C uses the wrong sign and amount. D mislabels the gap.

questions.question: Why can gross demand for x be positive while net demand for x is negative?

choices.A: Because the consumer may consume some x but own more x than they want to consume, so they sell the difference.
choices.B: Because negative net demand means the consumer dislikes x and consumes none.
choices.C: Because gross demand excludes endowments while net demand includes prices only.
choices.D: Because net demand is always negative in an endowment model.

questions.explanation: Gross demand is total desired consumption. Net demand subtracts the endowment. If endowed x exceeds desired consumption, gross demand is positive but net demand is negative. B falsely equates selling some with consuming none. C is muddled. D is false.

questions.question: A student receives 12 dining hall guest passes but wants to use only 8 and trade the rest for cash. Which concept describes the 8 passes, and which describes the 4 passes traded away?

choices.A: Gross demand is 8; net demand is -4.
choices.B: Gross demand is -4; net demand is 8.
choices.C: Gross demand is 12; net demand is 4.
choices.D: Gross demand is 4; net demand is 12.

questions.explanation: The student's gross demand is the total passes consumed, 8. Net demand is gross demand minus endowment, 8-12=-4, indicating net sale of 4. B reverses concepts. C confuses endowment with gross demand. D has both values wrong.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes gross demand from net demand?

choices.A: Gross demand is the total chosen quantity; net demand is total chosen quantity minus the initial endowment.
choices.B: Gross demand is quantity bought; net demand is quantity consumed.
choices.C: Gross demand is always positive; net demand is always zero.
choices.D: Gross demand applies to buyers; net demand applies only to firms.

questions.explanation: Gross demand is final consumption. Net demand is the trade amount relative to the endowment. B reverses buying and consuming. C is false. D incorrectly limits net demand.

questions.question: Ella has endowment omega_x=15. At price p_x=6, her gross demand is x=9. At price p_x=8, her gross demand is x=11. What happens to her net sale revenue from x, assuming these are the only relevant x trades?

choices.A: It falls from 36 to 32 because net supply falls from 6 to 4 while price rises.
choices.B: It rises from 54 to 88 because gross demand rises.
choices.C: It rises from 90 to 120 because endowment value rises.
choices.D: It falls from 6 to 4 because revenue equals net supply only.

questions.explanation: Net supply is omega_x-x. Initially 15-9=6, revenue 66=36. Finally 15-11=4, revenue 48=32. This is not full welfare analysis, but it is a numerical value calculation directly related to net trades. B uses gross demand. C uses endowment value rather than net sale revenue. D omits price.

topic: Labor Supply as an Endowment Problem

questions.question: Alex has 16 hours per day to divide between leisure L and labor H, with consumption C=wH. Utility is U(C,L)=C^{1/2}L^{1/2}. If w=20 and there is no nonlabor income, what are optimal leisure and labor hours?

choices.A: L=8 and H=8; equal Cobb-Douglas shares imply half full income spent on leisure and half on consumption.
choices.B: L=16 and H=0; leisure is free.
choices.C: L=0 and H=16; consumption requires labor.
choices.D: L=4 and H=12; labor is chosen until wage equals marginal utility.

questions.explanation: Full income is wT=2016=320. With equal shares, spending on leisure is wL=160, so L=8 and H=8. B ignores the need for consumption. C ignores the utility from leisure. D uses no correct tangency condition.

questions.question: Blair has T=12 hours, wage w=15, nonlabor income M=60, and utility U(C,L)=C^{1/2}L^{1/2}. The budget is C=w(T-L)+M. What are optimal leisure and labor?

choices.A: L=8 and H=4.
choices.B: L=6 and H=6.
choices.C: L=10 and H=2.
choices.D: L=4 and H=8.

questions.explanation: Full income is wT+M=15(12)+60=240. Equal Cobb-Douglas shares imply spending on leisure wL=120, so L=8 and H=4. B ignores nonlabor income. C overstates the effect of nonlabor income. D treats the wage as making leisure too expensive without applying the shares.

questions.question: Casey has U(C,L)=C^{1/2}L^{1/2}, T=16, and no nonlabor income. If the wage rises from 10 to 20, what happens to labor supply?

choices.A: Labor stays at 8 hours because both full income and the price of leisure double, leaving optimal leisure unchanged.
choices.B: Labor rises from 8 to 12 because leisure becomes more expensive.
choices.C: Labor falls from 8 to 4 because income doubles.
choices.D: Labor rises from 10 to 20 because wage doubles.

questions.explanation: With Cobb-Douglas equal shares and no nonlabor income, L=T/2=8 regardless of wage, so H=8. Substitution toward labor is exactly offset by the income effect toward leisure. B includes only substitution. C includes only income. D confuses wage with hours.

questions.question: Dana is initially working 6 hours and taking 10 hours leisure. A wage increase causes a substitution effect that reduces leisure by 3 hours and an income effect that increases leisure by 5 hours. What happens to labor supply, and why?

choices.A: Labor supply falls by 2 hours because the income effect toward leisure dominates the substitution effect away from leisure.
choices.B: Labor supply rises by 8 hours because both effects increase work.
choices.C: Labor supply rises by 2 hours because leisure rises by 2.
choices.D: Labor supply is unchanged because substitution and income effects always cancel.

questions.explanation: Leisure changes by -3+5=+2, so labor falls by 2. This is the backward-bending mechanism: at high wages, the income effect toward leisure can dominate. B adds absolute values. C gets the labor-leisure relationship backward. D assumes a special case.

questions.question: In a labor-leisure diagram with leisure L on the horizontal axis and consumption C on the vertical axis, a wage increase does what to the budget line?

choices.A: It makes the budget line steeper and rotates it around the full-time leisure endowment point if nonlabor income is unchanged.
choices.B: It makes the budget line flatter because leisure becomes cheaper.
choices.C: It shifts the budget line inward in parallel because work becomes more costly.
choices.D: It leaves the slope unchanged but raises the vertical intercept only.

questions.explanation: The wage is the opportunity cost of leisure and the slope magnitude of the budget line. A wage increase steepens the line. With the time endowment fixed, the line pivots around the point of full leisure plus nonlabor income. B reverses the price of leisure. C and D misdescribe the rotation.

questions.question: A labor supply curve first slopes upward and then bends backward at high wages. What does the backward-bending portion mean?

choices.A: Higher wages reduce hours worked because the income effect increasing desired leisure exceeds the substitution effect reducing leisure.
choices.B: Higher wages reduce leisure because leisure is inferior.
choices.C: Higher wages increase hours worked because substitution dominates income.
choices.D: The worker violates utility maximization.

questions.explanation: Backward bending means hours fall as wages rise. This happens when leisure is normal and the income effect toward more leisure dominates the substitution effect away from leisure. B says leisure falls, the opposite. C describes the upward-sloping region. D is false.

questions.question: Why can a wage increase make a worker choose more leisure?

choices.A: Because the wage increase raises full income; if leisure is normal, the income effect can increase desired leisure more than the substitution effect reduces it.
choices.B: Because leisure becomes cheaper when the wage rises.
choices.C: Because workers always target a fixed income.
choices.D: Because higher wages eliminate the budget constraint.

questions.explanation: A wage increase makes leisure more expensive, creating substitution away from leisure, but also makes the worker richer. If leisure is normal and the income effect dominates, leisure rises. B gets the opportunity cost wrong. C is too rigid. D is false.

questions.question: A nurse receives a large wage increase and decides to cut weekly shifts from 5 to 4 because she can maintain her target lifestyle with more time off. Which framework best explains this?

choices.A: Labor supply as an endowment problem with a dominant income effect for leisure.
choices.B: Giffen demand for labor, because labor demand rises with wages.
choices.C: WARP violation, because the nurse chose fewer hours at a higher wage.
choices.D: Perfect complements, because work and leisure are consumed together.

questions.explanation: The worker is endowed with time and sells labor. A higher wage creates substitution and income effects; fewer hours means the income effect toward leisure dominates. B confuses labor supply with labor demand. C mistakes changed choices for inconsistency. D is not implied.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes leisure demand from labor supply?

choices.A: Leisure demand is hours not worked; labor supply is the time endowment minus leisure demand.
choices.B: Leisure demand is bought in markets; labor supply is consumed directly.
choices.C: Leisure demand rises with wages only when labor supply also rises.
choices.D: Leisure demand and labor supply are the same variable.

questions.explanation: With time endowment T, labor supply is H=T-L. B reverses consumption and sale. C misses the inverse relationship between leisure and labor. D ignores the subtraction from total time.

questions.question: A worker's labor supply is H=2w for wages from 10 to 20. If the wage rises from 10 to 20, what is the increase in worker surplus measured under this labor supply curve?

choices.A: 300; hours rise from 20 to 40, so gain is 10(20)+0.5(10)(20).
choices.B: 200; old hours 20 times wage increase 10.
choices.C: 100; triangle only, 0.5(10)(20).
choices.D: 400; new hours 40 times wage increase 10.

questions.explanation: At w=10, H=20; at w=20, H=40. The surplus gain from the wage increase is the rectangle on old hours, 1020=200, plus the triangle on additional hours, 0.51020=100, totaling 300. B omits the triangle. C omits the rectangle. D treats all new earnings as surplus gain.
